abstract: While the mission of Jesus in Judea (Jn 3:22–36) is often overlooked in contemporary scholarship, its geographical placement is central to understanding the Gospel’s narrative trajectory. This study examines the spatial structure of John 2:13–4:54, suggesting it follows a pattern analogous to the missional movement found in Acts 1:8. In the Johannine account, Jesus’ ministry shifts from the religious center of Jerusalem toward the Judean countryside, subsequently reaching Samaria and Galilee. This movement from the center to the periphery ensures that all segments of Israel are presented with the message of faith prior to the escalating conflicts with authorities detailed in later chapters. Within this geographical framework, the identity of the anonymous "Jew" (Ἰουδαῖος) in John 3:25—who is engaged in a dispute with the disciples of John the Baptist—takes on new significance. By situating the term within the specific "land of Judea," this paper argues that Ἰουδαῖος should be interpreted primarily as a "Judean," reflecting a regional rather than a strictly religious designation. Such a reading provides clarity to the verse and integrates the episode more firmly into Jesus’ systematic mission to the whole of Israel.
